title: Cumulative geometric probability
description: Practice calculating the probability that the first success occurs some time before or after a certain trial number.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jeremiah makes $25\%$ of the three-point shots he attempts. For a warm up, Jeremiah likes to shoot three-point shots until he makes one. Let $M$ be the number of shots it takes Jeremiah to make his first three-point shot. Assume that the results of each shot are independent.

**Find the probability that it takes Jeremiah more than $6$ attempts to make his first shot.**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

$P(M>6)=$  [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Without a fancy calculator

On each shot:

$P(\blueD{\text{make}})=0.25$  
$P(\purpleD{\text{miss}})=0.75$  

If the first time he makes the shot occurs after his $6^{\text{th}}$ attempt, then he needs to miss his first $6$ shots.

$\begin{align}
P(M>6)&=P(\text{miss first 6}) \\\\
&=(\purpleD{0.75})^6 \\\\
&\approx0.178
\end{align}$

###With a fancy calculator

We can use the geometric cumulative density function on most graphing calculators to find this probability. Some calculators will only compute cumulative probability as *less than or equal to* a given value, so we can't use our calculator to directly find $P(M>6)$ (it takes Jeremiah more than $6$ attempts to make his first shot), but we can use the calculator to find the complement, which is $P(M\leq6)$ (it takes Jeremiah $6$ or less attempts), and subtract that probability from $1$:

$P(M>6)=1-P(M\leq6)$

On TI-84, press the "2nd" key followed by "distr" (above the "vars" key) to access the distributions menu. From there, we choose "G: geometcdf" and complete these inputs:

$\begin{align}
&\text{geometcdf} \\\\
&\text{p: }0.25 \\\\
&\text{x value: }6
\end{align}$

The output is approximately $0.8220$, which is $P(M\leq6)$.

$P(M>6)\approx1-0.8220\approx0.1780$

[[☃ explanation 3]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]
$P(M>6)\approx0.178 \approx 0.18$

assessment_items.question_content:
Jeremiah makes $25\%$ of the three-point shots he attempts. For a warm up, Jeremiah likes to shoot three-point shots until he makes one.  Let $M$ be the number of shots it takes Jeremiah to make his first three-point shot. Assume that the results of each shot are independent.

**Find the probability that it takes Jeremiah fewer than $4$ attempts to make his first shot.**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

$P(M<4)=$  [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Without a fancy calculator

On each shot:

$P(\blueD{\text{make}})=0.25$  
$P(\purpleD{\text{miss}})=0.75$  

If it takes Jeremiah fewer than $4$ attempts to make his first shot, here are the possible sequences of shots:

* make
* miss, make
* miss, miss, make

We can find the probability of each sequence and add those probabilities together.

$\begin{align}
P(\blueD{\text{make}})&=0.25 \\\\\\
P(\purpleD{\text{miss}}, \blueD{\text{make}})&=(\purpleD{0.75})(\blueD{0.25})\\\\&=0.1875 \\\\\\
P(\purpleD{\text{miss}}, \purpleD{\text{miss}}, \blueD{\text{make}} )&=(\purpleD{0.75})(\purpleD{0.75})(\blueD{0.25})
\\\\&=0.140625 \\\\\\
P(M<4)&=0.25+0.1875+0.140625
\\\\&=0.578125
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###With a fancy calculator

We can use the geometric cumulative density function on most graphing calculators to find this probability. Some calculators will only compute cumulative probability as *less than or equal to* a given value. The probability we want $P(M<4)$ (it takes Jeremiah fewer than $4$ attempts to make his first shot) is equivalent to $P(M\leq3)$ (it takes him $3$ or less attempts to make his first shot).

On TI-84, press the "2nd" key followed by "distr" (above the "vars" key) to access the distributions menu. From there, we choose "G: geometcdf" and complete these inputs:

$\begin{align}
&\text{geometcdf} \\\\
&\text{p: }0.25 \\\\
&\text{x value: }3
\end{align}$

The output is $0.578125$, which is $P(M\leq3)$.

[[☃ explanation 3]]

[[☃ explanation 4]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Zach scores a ringer $40\%$ of the time that he throws a horseshoe. Let $R$ be the number of throws it takes Zach to score his first ringer in a game. Assume the results of each throw are independent.

**Find the probability that it takes Zach $5$ or more throws to score his first ringer.**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

$P(R\geq 5)=$  [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Without a fancy calculator

On each throw:

$P(\blueD{\text{ringer}})=0.4$  
$P(\purpleD{\text{no ringer}})=0.6$  

If it takes Zach $5$ or more throws to score his first ringer, then does not make a ringer on his first $4$ throws.

$\begin{align}
P(R\geq5)&=P(\text{no ringer first 4}) \\\\
&=(\purpleD{0.6})^4 \\\\
&= 0.1296
\end{align}$

###With a fancy calculator

We can use the geometric cumulative density function on most graphing calculators to find this probability. Some calculators will only compute cumulative probability as *less than or equal to* a given value, so we can't use our calculator to directly find $P(R\geq5)$ (it takes Zach $5$ or more throws to score his first ringer), but we can use the calculator to find the complement, which is $P(R\leq4)$ (it takes him $4$ or less attempts), and subtract that probability from $1$:

$P(R\geq5)=1-P(R\leq4)$

On TI-84, press the "2nd" key followed by "distr" (above the "vars" key) to access the distributions menu. From there, we choose "G: geometcdf" and complete these inputs:

$\begin{align}
&\text{geometcdf} \\\\
&\text{p: }0.4 \\\\
&\text{x value: }4
\end{align}$

The output is $0.8704$, which is $P(R\leq4)$.

$P(R\geq5)=1-0.8704=0.1296$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Zach scores a ringer $40\%$ of the time that he throws a horseshoe. Let $R$ be the number of throws it takes Zach to score his first ringer in a game. Assume the results of each throw are independent.

**Find the probability that it takes Zach $2$ or fewer throws to score his first ringer.**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

$P(R\leq 2)=$  [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Without a fancy calculator

On each throw:

$P(\blueD{\text{ringer}})=0.4$  
$P(\purpleD{\text{no ringer}})=0.6$  

If it takes Zach $2$ or fewer throws to score his first ringer, here are the possible sequences of throws:

- ringer
- no ringer, ringer

We can find the probability of each sequence and add those probabilities together.

$\begin{align}
P(\blueD{\text{ringer}}) &= \blueD{0.4}\\\\\\
P(\purpleD{\text{no ringer}},\blueD{\text{ringer}}) &= (\purpleD{0.6})(\blueD{0.4})\\\\&=0.24\\\\\\
P(R\leq2) &= 0.4+0.24\\\\&=0.64
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###With a fancy calculator

We can use the geometric cumulative density function on most graphing calculators to find this probability. Some calculators will only compute cumulative probability as *less than or equal to* a given value, which is what we want in this problem (it asked for $P(R\leq2)$, or the probability that it takes Zach $2$ or less throws to score his first ringer).

On TI-84, press the "2nd" key followed by "distr" (above the "vars" key) to access the distributions menu. From there, we choose "G: geometcdf" and complete these inputs:

$\begin{align}
&\text{geometcdf} \\\\
&\text{p: }0.4 \\\\
&\text{x value: }2
\end{align}$

The output is $0.64$, which is $P(R\leq2)$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Emelia registers vehicles for the Department of Transportation. Sports utility vehicles (SUVs) make up $12\%$ of the vehicles she registers. Let $V$ be the number of vehicles Emelia registers in a day until she first registers an SUV. Assume the type of each vehicle is independent.

**Find the probability that Emelia registers more than $4$ vehicles before she registers an SUV.**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

$P(V>4)=$  [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Without a fancy calculator

For each vehicle:

$P(\blueD{\text{SUV}})=0.12$  
$P(\purpleD{\text{not}})=0.88$  

If Emelia registers more than $4$ vehicles before she registers an SUV, then the first $4$ vehicles must not be SUVs.

$\begin{align}
P(V>4)&=P(\text{4 not SUVs}) \\\\
&=(\purpleD{0.88})^4 \\\\
&\approx 0.5997
\end{align}$

###With a fancy calculator

We can use the geometric cumulative density function on most graphing calculators to find this probability. Some calculators will only compute cumulative probability as *less than or equal to* a given value, so we can't use our calculator to directly find $P(V>4)$ (it takes more than $4$ vehicles until Emelia registers the first SUV that day), but we can use the calculator to find the complement, which is $P(V\leq4)$ (it takes $4$ or less vehicles), and subtract that probability from $1$:

$P(V>4)=1-P(V\leq4)$

On TI-84, press the "2nd" key followed by "distr" (above the "vars" key) to access the distributions menu. From there, we choose "G: geometcdf" and complete these inputs:

$\begin{align}
&\text{geometcdf} \\\\
&\text{p: }0.12 \\\\
&\text{x value: }4
\end{align}$

The output is approximately $0.4003$, which is $P(V\leq4)$.

$P(V>4)\approx1-0.4003\approx0.5997$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]
$P(V>4)\approx 0.5997 \approx 0.6$

assessment_items.question_content:
Hadassah bowls a strike on $60\%$ of the frames she bowls. Let $F$ be the number of frames Hadassah bowls until she bowls a strike. Assume the results of each frame are independent.

**Find the probability that it takes Hadassah less than $3$ frames to bowl her first strike.**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

$P(F<3)=$  [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Without a fancy calculator

On each frame:

$P(\blueD{\text{strike}})=0.6$  
$P(\purpleD{\text{not}})=0.4$  

If it takes Hadassah less than $3$ frames to bowl her first strike, here are the possible sequences of frames:

* strike
* not, strike

We can find the probability of each sequence and add those probabilities together.

$\begin{align}
P(\blueD{\text{strike}})&=0.6\\\\\\
P(\purpleD{\text{not}}, \blueD{\text{strike}})&=(\purpleD{0.4})(\blueD{0.6})\\\\&=0.24\\\\\\
P(F<3)&=0.6+0.24\\\\&=0.84
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###With a fancy calculator

We can use the geometric cumulative density function on most graphing calculators to find this probability. Some calculators will only compute cumulative probability as *less than or equal to* a given value. The probability we want $P(F<3)$ (it takes Hadassah less than $3$ frames to bowl her first strike) is equivalent to $P(F\leq2)$ (it takes her $2$ or less attempts to bowl her first strike).

On TI-84, press the "2nd" key followed by "distr" (above the "vars" key) to access the distributions menu. From there, we choose "G: geometcdf" and complete these inputs:

$\begin{align}
&\text{geometcdf} \\\\
&\text{p: }0.6 \\\\
&\text{x value: }2
\end{align}$

The output is $0.84$, which is $P(F\leq2)$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Anand knows from experience that if he does not review a new vocabulary word that he has learned, that he has a $70\%$ chance of forgetting it each day. Let $D$ be the number of days Anand goes without reviewing a word until he forgets it.

**Find the probability that it takes Anand $4$ or more days to forget the word.**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

$P(D\geq 4)=$  [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Without a fancy calculator

On each day:

$P(\blueD{\text{forget}})=0.7$  
$P(\purpleD{\text{remember}})=0.3$  

If it takes Anand $4$ or more days to forget the word, then he must remember for each of the first $3$ days.

$\begin{align}
P(D\geq 4)&=P(\text{remember first 3}) \\\\
&=(\purpleD{0.3})^3 \\\\
&= 0.027
\end{align}$

###With a fancy calculator

We can use the geometric cumulative density function on most graphing calculators to find this probability. Some calculators will only compute cumulative probability as *less than or equal to* a given value, so we can't use our calculator to directly find $P(D\geq4)$ (it takes Anand $4$ or more days to forget the word), but we can use the calculator to find the complement, which is $P(D\leq3)$ (it takes him $3$ or less days), and subtract that probability from $1$:

$P(D\geq4)=1-P(D\leq3)$

On TI-84, press the "2nd" key followed by "distr" (above the "vars" key) to access the distributions menu. From there, we choose "G: geometcdf" and complete these inputs:

$\begin{align}
&\text{geometcdf} \\\\
&\text{p: }0.7 \\\\
&\text{x value: }3
\end{align}$

The output is $0.973$, which is $P(D\leq3)$.

$P(D\geq4)=1-0.973=0.027$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Minenhle registers vehicles for the Department of Transportation. Leased vehicles make up $6\%$ of the vehicles she registers. Let $V$ be the number of vehicles Minenhle registers in a day until she first registers a leased vehicle. Assume the status of each vehicle is independent.

**Find the probability that Minenhle registers a leased vehicle within the first $3$ vehicles of the day.**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

$P(V \leq 3)=$  [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Without a fancy calculator

For each vehicle:

$P(\blueD{\text{leased}})=0.06$  
$P(\purpleD{\text{not}})=0.94$ 

If Minenhle first registers a leased vehicle in the first $3$ vehicles of the day, here are the possible sequences of vehicles:

- leased
- not, leased
- not, not, leased

We can find the probability of each sequence and add those probabilities together.

$\begin{align}
P(\blueD{\text{leased}}) &= \blueD{0.06}\\\\\\
P(\purpleD{\text{not}},\blueD{\text{leased}}) &= (\purpleD{0.94})(\blueD{0.06})\\\\&=0.0564\\\\\\
P(\purpleD{\text{not}},\purpleD{\text{not}},\blueD{\text{leased}}) &= (\purpleD{0.94})^2(\blueD{0.06})\\\\&=0.053016\\\\\\
P(V\leq 3) &= 0.06+0.0564+0.053016 \\\\&=0.169416
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###With a fancy calculator

We can use the geometric cumulative density function on most graphing calculators to find this probability. Some calculators will only compute cumulative probability as *less than or equal to* a given value, which is what we want in this problem (it asked for $P(V\leq3)$, or the probability that Minenhle registers a leased vehicle within the first $3$ vehicles of the day).

On TI-84, press the "2nd" key followed by "distr" (above the "vars" key) to access the distributions menu. From there, we choose "G: geometcdf" and complete these inputs:

$\begin{align}
&\text{geometcdf} \\\\
&\text{p: }0.06 \\\\
&\text{x value: }3
\end{align}$

The output is $0.169416$, which is $P(V\leq3)$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]
$P(V \leq 3) \approx 0.17$

assessment_items.question_content:
Nuan is using a faulty barcode scanner that misreads barcodes $5\%$ of the time. Let $B$ = the number of barcodes Nuan scans until the scanner misreads one. Assume the success of each scan is independent.

**Find the probability that Nuan scans greater than $10$ barcodes before the scanner misreads one.**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

$P(B>10)=$  [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Without a fancy calculator

For each barcode scanned:

$P(\blueD{\text{misread}})=0.05$  
$P(\purpleD{\text{read correctly}})=0.95$  

If Nuan scans greater than $10$ barcodes before the scanner misreads one, then the scanner must read the first $10$ barcodes correctly.

$\begin{align}
P(B>10)&=P(10\text{ read correctly}) \\\\
&=(\purpleD{0.95})^{10} \\\\
&\approx 0.5987
\end{align}$

###With a fancy calculator

We can use the geometric cumulative density function on most graphing calculators to find this probability. Some calculators will only compute cumulative probability as *less than or equal to* a given value, so we can't use our calculator to directly find $P(B>10)$ (Nuan scans greater than $10$ barcodes before the scanner misreads one), but we can use the calculator to find the complement, which is $P(B\leq10)$ (he scans $10$ or less before the scanner misreads one), and subtract that probability from $1$:

$P(B>10)=1-P(B\leq10)$

On TI-84, press the "2nd" key followed by "distr" (above the "vars" key) to access the distributions menu. From there, we choose "G: geometcdf" and complete these inputs:

$\begin{align}
&\text{geometcdf} \\\\
&\text{p: }0.05 \\\\
&\text{x value: }10
\end{align}$

The output is approximately $0.4013$, which is $P(B\leq10)$.

$P(B>10)\approx1-0.4013\approx0.5987$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]
$P(B>10)\approx 0.5987 \approx 0.6$

assessment_items.question_content:
Lilyana runs a cake decorating business, for which $10\%$ of her orders come over the telephone. Let $C$ be the number of cake orders Lilyana receives in a month until she first gets an order over the telephone. Assume the method of placing each cake order is independent.

**Find the probability that it takes fewer than $5$ orders for Lilyana to get her first telephone order of the month.**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

$P(C<5)=$  [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Without a fancy calculator

On each order:

$P(\blueD{\text{telephone}})=0.1$  
$P(\purpleD{\text{other}})=0.9$  

If it takes fewer than $5$ orders for Lilyana to get her first phone order of the month, here are the possible sequences of orders:

* phone
* other, phone
* other, other, phone
* other, other, other, phone

We could find the probability of each sequence and add those probabilities together. However, it would be faster to take the complement of the probability that none of the first $4$ orders is a phone order.

$\begin{align}
P(C<5) &= 1-P(4\text{ ordered other ways})\\\\
&=1-(\purpleD{0.9})^4 \\\\
&=1-0.6561\\\\
&=0.3439
\end{align}$


[[☃ explanation 1]]

###With a fancy calculator

We can use the geometric cumulative density function on most graphing calculators to find this probability. Some calculators will only compute cumulative probability as *less than or equal to* a given value. The probability we want $P(C<5)$ (it takes fewer than $5$ orders for Lilyana to get her first telephone order of the month) is equivalent to $P(C\leq4)$ (it takes $4$ or less orders).

On TI-84, press the "2nd" key followed by "distr" (above the "vars" key) to access the distributions menu. From there, we choose "G: geometcdf" and complete these inputs:

$\begin{align}
&\text{geometcdf} \\\\
&\text{p: }0.1 \\\\
&\text{x value: }4
\end{align}$

The output is $0.3439$, which is $P(C\leq4)$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

assessment_items.question_content:
On average, $15\%$ of the patients a certain emergency room receives are uninsured. Let $N$ be the number of patients the emergency room receives until it receives the first uninsured patient of the day. Assume the insurance status of the patients is independent.

**Find the probability that it takes at least $6$ patients until the emergency room receives the first uninsured patient.**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

$P(N\geq 6)=$  [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Without a fancy calculator

For each patient:

$P(\blueD{\text{uninsured}})=0.15$  
$P(\purpleD{\text{insured}})=0.85$  

If it takes at least $6$ patients before the emergency room receives the first uninsured patient, then the first $5$ patients must be insured.

$\begin{align}
P(N\geq 6)&=P(5\text{ insured}) \\\\
&=(\purpleD{0.85})^5 \\\\
&= 0.4437053125
\end{align}$

###With a fancy calculator

We can use the geometric cumulative density function on most graphing calculators to find this probability. Some calculators will only compute cumulative probability as *less than or equal to* a given value, so we can't use our calculator to directly find $P(N\geq6)$ (it takes at least $6$ patients until the emergency room receives the first uninsured patient), but we can use the calculator to find the complement, which is $P(N\leq5)$ (it takes $5$ or less patients), and subtract that probability from $1$:

$P(N\geq6)=1-P(N\leq5)$

On TI-84, press the "2nd" key followed by "distr" (above the "vars" key) to access the distributions menu. From there, we choose "G: geometcdf" and complete these inputs:

$\begin{align}
&\text{geometcdf} \\\\
&\text{p: }0.15 \\\\
&\text{x value: }5
\end{align}$

The output is approximately $0.5563$, which is $P(N\leq5)$.

$P(N\geq6)\approx1-0.5563\approx0.4437$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]
$P(N\geq 6) \approx0.44$

assessment_items.question_content:
An airline offers a survey for its passengers to complete after every flight. Each time a passenger completes the survey, there is a $2\%$ chance they will win a discounted price on their next flight. Assume that winners are selected at random, and the results of the surveys are independent.

Zaylee has numerous trips planned with this airline, and she'll always complete each survey in hopes of winning. Let $N$ be the number of surveys Zaylee completes until she wins for the first time.

**Find the probability that it takes Zaylee $3$ surveys or less to win for the first time.**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

$P(N \leq 3)=$  [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Without a fancy calculator

For each survey:

$\begin{align}
P(\blueD{\text{win}})&=0.02\\\\
P(\purpleD{\text{not}})&=0.98
\end{align}$ 

If it takes Zaylee $3$ surveys or less to win for the first time, here are the possible sequences of results:

- win
- not, win
- not, not, win

We can find the probability of each sequence and add those probabilities together.

$\begin{align}
P(\blueD{\text{win}}) &= \blueD{0.02}\\\\\\
P(\purpleD{\text{not}},\blueD{\text{win}}) &= \left(\purpleD{0.98}\right)\left(\blueD{0.02}\right)\\\\
&=0.0196\\\\\\
P(\purpleD{\text{not}},\purpleD{\text{not}},\blueD{\text{win}}) &= \left(\purpleD{0.98}\right)^2\left(\blueD{0.02}\right)\\\\
&=0.019208\\\\\\
P(N\leq 3) &= 0.02+0.0196+0.019208 \\\\
&=0.058808
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###With a fancy calculator

We can use the geometric cumulative density function on most graphing calculators to find this probability. Some calculators will only compute cumulative probability as *less than or equal to* a given value, which is what we want in this problem (it asked for $P(N\leq3)$, or the probability that it takes Zaylee $3$ surveys or less to win for the first time).

On TI-84, press the "2nd" key followed by "distr" (above the "vars" key) to access the distributions menu. From there, we choose "G: geometcdf" and complete these inputs:

$\begin{align}
&\text{geometcdf} \\\\
&\text{p: }0.02 \\\\
&\text{x value: }3
\end{align}$

The output is $0.058808$, which is $P(N\leq3)$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]
$P(N \leq 3) \approx 0.06$
